channel.title: NYT > U.S. News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: U.S. to Review Mexican Consulates After Right-Wing Claims Against Them
item.description: The State Department said it would review all Mexican consulates in the United States, following claims in conservative media that the consulates interfere in politics.
item.dc:creator: Jack Nicas
item.media:credit: Alisha Jucevic for The New York Times
item.media:description: The State Department is reviewing the 53 Mexican consulates in the U.S., including the consulate in Los Angeles.

item.title: Jason Meade Found Guilty in Fatal Shooting of Casey Goodson Jr. in Ohio
item.description: Jason Meade had been searching for a fugitive when he shot Casey Goodson Jr. in 2020. It is rare for a law enforcement official to be convicted in a killing that occurs on the job.
item.dc:creator: Soumya Karlamangla
item.media:description: Jason Meade in court in Columbus, Ohio, on Wednesday.

item.title: State Department Will Revoke Passports of Parents Who Owe Child Support
item.description: The Trump administration is moving to more strictly enforce a 1996 law that gives the State Department the authority to revoke passports over unpaid child support.
item.dc:creator: Chris Cameron
item.media:description: It is unclear how many citizens could have their passports revoked under the policy.

item.title: Alaska Can Resume Killing Bears to Protect Caribou Herd, Judge Says
item.description: The state program employs helicopters and small planes to shoot black and brown bears of any age in a large area of southwestern Alaska. Two conservation groups have sued to stop it.
item.dc:creator: John S.W. MacDonald
item.media:credit: Acacia Johnson for The New York Times
item.media:description: Brown bears in Alaska. The state culls bears in the spring, during the caribou calving season. Nearly 200 bears were killed between 2023 and 2025, according to a lawsuit from two conservation groups.

item.title: Trump Reversed Hormuz Plan After Saudis Denied Airspace Access
item.description: Saudi Arabia’s refusal of support suggests that President Trump’s unpredictable approach to Iran has strained ties with one of his closest allies in the Middle East.
item.dc:creator: Michael Crowley, Eric Schmitt and Vivian Nereim
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman of Saudi Arabia at the White House with President Trump in November.

item.title: Fetterman Says He Has ‘No Plans’ to Leave the Democratic Party
item.description: In an opinion piece in The Washington Post, the Pennsylvania senator wrote that while he was “at odds” with the party’s approach to some issues, his values had not changed.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.media:description: Senator John Fetterman, Democrat of Pennsylvania, wrote in an opinion piece in The Washington Post this week that the Democratic Party had veered off course in its stance on immigration and the war in Iran.

item.title: U.S. Strikes Iranian Targets; Iran Says It Returned Fire
item.description: The exchange was the latest twist in a week of mixed signals in the region and tensions over the Strait of Hormuz, as President Trump searches for an off-ramp in the war that he started.
item.dc:creator: Helene Cooper and Eric Schmitt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Vessels in the Strait of Hormuz near Musandam, Oman, on Wednesday.

item.title: Louisiana Asks Supreme Court to Halt Abortion Pill Access by Mail
item.description: The request comes after a federal appeals court temporarily blocked a Food and Drug Administration regulation that has greatly expanded access to the abortion pill mifepristone.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow
item.media:credit: Hannah Yoon for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mifepristone is typically delivered as part of a two-drug regimen through the first 12 weeks of pregnancy.

item.title: Millions for a Paint Job? Trump Wants the Eisenhower Building Painted White.
item.description: The project would transform the granite exterior of the historic Eisenhower Executive Office Building, which dates to the 1800s.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater
item.media:credit: Nathan Howard for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Eisenhower building was built for the State, War and Navy Departments between 1871 and 1888, and now houses hundreds of federal workers.

item.title: F.B.I. Says Austin Bar Shooter Was Driven by Personal Grievances Related to Iran War
item.description: Investigators said the attacker admired Iran’s leadership and had no ties to a foreign terrorist organization.
item.dc:creator: Jesus Jiménez, Lauren McGaughy and Ramón Ramirez
item.media:description: A gunman who killed three people and injured 15 in an Austin bar shooting in March acted alone.

item.title: Special Counsel to Investigate Trump Administration Lawyer for Possible Misconduct
item.description: Federal judges in Rhode Island ordered an investigation into a lawyer who withheld information about a migrant’s international criminal charges. Now, D.H.S. says it can’t find the migrant.
item.dc:creator: Mattathias Schwartz
item.media:credit: Valerie Plesch for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Rhode Island incident is the latest in a string of immigration enforcement cases in which judges have expressed anger with administration lawyers, particularly as they have provided information from D.H.S. officials.

item.title: Anthony James Kazmierczak Pleads Guilty in Syringe Attack Against Rep. Ilhan Omar
item.description: The attack happened in late January during a town-hall meeting in Minneapolis, at the height of the Trump administration’s immigration crackdown in Minnesota.
item.dc:creator: Ernesto Londoño
item.media:credit: Victor J. Blue for The New York Times
item.media:description: Security officials restrain Anthony James Kazmierczak during a town hall in Minneapolis in January.

item.title: ICE Arrests Violated Order Requiring Warrants in D.C., Judge Rules
item.description: Despite a previous order, the administration has continued to follow guidance from January empowering immigrations agents to widely make arrests without probable cause, judge says.
item.dc:creator: Zach Montague
item.media:description: Federal law enforcement agents and Washington police officers conducting a traffic stop in August.

item.title: As Stockpiles Fall, U.S. Sells More Missiles Worth $17 Billion to Gulf Nations
item.description: The United States and its partners have burned through an enormous amount of air defense missiles in the Iran war, and production is slow.
item.dc:creator: Edward Wong
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: A Patriot missile launcher in Kuwait City in 2019. The United States is selling Kuwait another $9.3 billion worth of interceptor missiles.

item.title: Mohamed Sabry Soliman Is Sentenced to Life in Prison for Colorado Attack
item.description: Mohamed Sabry Soliman pleaded guilty to 101 charges, including one count of murder, after the firebombing attack in Colorado last June.
item.dc:creator: Kelley Manley and Jack Healy
item.media:credit: Michael Ciaglo for The New York Times
item.media:description: Police officers outside a Stand Against Antisemitism and Hate event last June at the site of the attack in Boulder, Colo.

item.title: Appeals Court Looks Unlikely to Allow Hegseth to Punish Mark Kelly for Video
item.description: A three-judge panel in Washington heard arguments in the lawsuit aimed at stopping the Pentagon from disciplining Senator Mark Kelly for a video warning about illegal military orders.
item.dc:creator: Megan Mineiro
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: “If you say something that the president, or this administration, does not like, they’re going to come after you,” Senator Mark Kelly, Democrat of Arizona, said at a news conference after the hearing on Thursday.

item.title: Tennessee Approves New Map Aimed at Flipping the Last Democratic Seat
item.description: After a Supreme Court ruling that weakened the Voting Rights Act of 1965, Republicans carved up a majority-Black Memphis seat as the national redistricting wars continue.
item.dc:creator: Emily Cochrane
item.media:credit: Brad J. Vest for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Steve Cohen, left, Tennessee State Senator Raumesh Akbari, center, and Tennessee State Senator London Lamar, right, discuss the new House map unveiled by Tennessee Republicans on Wednesday.

item.title: Trump Administration Weighs Citing ‘Emergency’ to Admit More White South Africans as Refugees
item.description: The move would further solidify the U.S. refugee program as a pipeline for the white minority from South Africa.
item.dc:creator: Zolan Kanno-Youngs, Hamed Aleaziz and Miriam Jordan
item.media:description: White South Africans arriving in Virginia last year after having been granted refugee status by the Trump administration.

item.title: In L.A. Mayor’s Race, Karen Bass Faces Vocal Rivals and a ‘Disgruntled Electorate’
item.description: The mayor of Los Angeles is running for a second term as a former ally and a reality TV star tap into widespread voter angst.
item.dc:creator: Shawn Hubler, Jill Cowan and Conor Dougherty
item.media:description: Mayor Karen Bass never fully recovered from her absence from California when Pacific Palisades erupted in flames last year.

item.title: House Republican Proposes Bill to Wind Down the Iran War
item.description: Representative Tom Barrett, a Michigan Republican facing a tough re-election race, introduced a bill to impose limits on the use of military force in Iran and end the fighting this summer.
item.dc:creator: Robert Jimison
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Alfield Reeves for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Tom Barrett at a rally in 2024.

item.title: Graham Platner and Susan Collins Duel in High-Stakes Maine Senate Race Ads
item.description: In strikingly different ads, Graham Platner argued that “Susan Collins’s charade is over” while Senator Collins highlighted the economic benefits she has brought to Maine.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Lerer
item.media:description: The race in Maine between Graham Platner, a Democrat, and Senator Susan Collins, a Republican, could decide control of the Senate.

item.title: Did This Appeals Court Go Rogue on Abortion Pills?
item.description: The Fifth Circuit, reversed more than any other appeals court, has a reputation for taking extreme positions.
item.dc:creator: Adam Liptak
item.media:credit: Jackie Molloy for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mifepristone and Misoprostol pills.

item.title: Why Trump’s Feud With the Pope Worries Republicans
item.description: Our national political correspondent Lisa Lerer explains the impact of President Trump’s spate of attacks against Pope Leo XIV ahead of the closely contested midterm elections.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Lerer, Alexandra Ostasiewicz, Nikolay Nikolov, Edward Vega and Rafaela Balster

item.title: Charlotte, N.C. Mayor Vi Lyles Says She Will Resign in May
item.description: Mayor Vi Lyles did not fully explain why she would leave office early, saying only that she wanted to spend more time with family.
item.dc:creator: Eduardo Medina
item.media:description: Mayor Vi Lyles announced on Thursday that she would resign next month.

item.title: Federal and State Officials Discuss Closing Florida’s ‘Alligator Alcatraz’
item.description: The immigrant detention center in the Everglades, which Gov. Ron DeSantis, a Republican, opened last July, may be too expensive to keep operating.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Mazzei and Hamed Aleaziz
item.media:description: President Trump and Kristi Noem, the homeland security secretary at the time, toured the immigration center known as Alligator Alcatraz with Gov. Ron DeSantis of Florida and James Uthmeier, the state’s attorney general, last July.

item.title: Rubio Meets Pope Leo Amid Growing Tensions Between Trump and Vatican
item.description: The meeting at the Vatican followed President Trump’s condemnation of Pope Leo XIV for opposing the war in Iran.
item.dc:creator: Motoko Rich
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Pope Leo XIV meeting with Secretary of State Marco Rubio at the Vatican in a photograph released Thursday by Vatican Media.

item.title: Ted Turner’s Mark on Atlanta Lived On, Even After He Left the Spotlight
item.description: The city where he planted his empire came to embody his ambitions and his confidence. Mr. Turner died on Wednesday at 87.
item.dc:creator: Rick Rojas
item.media:description: Ted Turner shows off his World Series Championship ring before the Atlanta Braves’ season-opening game in 1995.

item.title: Florida Creates a More Conservative Course to Rival A.P. History
item.description: Here are ways Florida’s advanced high school history program, which students can use for college credit, differs from other curriculums.
item.dc:creator: Dana Goldstein
item.media:credit: Nicole Craine for The New York Times
item.media:description: Gov. Ron DeSantis has pushed forward a more conservative agenda for Florida’s schools, including through criticism of Advanced Placement courses.

item.title: What Are ‘Teen Takeovers’ and Why Are Police Struggling to Stop Them?
item.description: Across the country, police and city officials are trying to crack down on sometimes violent youth gatherings, but the teens themselves say they need some way to socialize and blow off steam.
item.dc:creator: Clyde McGrady, Emma Schartz and Julie Bosman
item.media:credit: Jamie Kelter Davis for The New York Times
item.media:description: Police officers detained a young person after chasing him down a street amid rumors of a “teen takeover” in the Hyde Park neighborhood of Chicago last month.
